name: Broken Authentication
what_it_means: Identity verification is weak or incorrect: APIs accept missing, expired, or tampered tokens; sessions or keys can be replayed, swapped, or forged — allowing attackers to impersonate legitimate users or gain unauthorized access without valid credentials.
animated_example: Security gate where guards wave everyone through without checking IDs — anyone who shows up, real or fake, gets in.
quick_checks: All protected endpoints require valid, unexpired tokens (OAuth2/OIDC/JWT)
Token signature/claims validated (iss, aud, exp, kid); alg:none explicitly rejected
Short-lived access tokens (≤15 min); rotating refresh tokens; revocation honored immediately
MFA for sensitive actions; device binding where appropriate
TLS enforced on all connections; no credentials in URLs or log output
Brute-force and credential-stuffing protections on all auth endpoints
Default/weak credentials disallowed; API keys rotated on a fixed schedule
Consistent 401 for missing/invalid token; 403 for valid but unauthorized

payload_types.description: Attacker exploits weak JWT algorithm validation by switching to alg:none to bypass signature checks entirely, or swapping RS256 to HS256 and re-signing the token with the server's public key as the HMAC secret.
examples: Sending a JWT with header {"alg":"none"} and no signature — server accepts it as valid
Changing alg from RS256 to HS256 and re-signing with the server's known RSA public key as the HMAC secret
Setting "kid":"../../dev/null" in the JWT header to force empty-key HMAC verification
Submitting an expired token with the alg field modified to none to bypass expiry check
Crafting a JWT with alg:HS256 and signing with an empty string to exploit servers that use empty-string fallback secrets
mitigation: Explicitly whitelist accepted signing algorithms (RS256, ES256) in the JWT validation library — never allow the algorithm to be determined by the token header alone.
Unconditionally reject tokens with alg:none or alg:null regardless of any other claim values.
Validate the kid header against a pre-registered, server-controlled keyset — reject any kid that resolves to a path, URL, or value not in the whitelist.
Use a well-maintained JWT library with algorithm pinning (e.g., jose, python-jose, nimbus-jose-jwt) rather than implementing validation manually.
Rotate signing keys on a regular schedule and immediately invalidate tokens signed with revoked key IDs.

payload_types.type: Token Replay After Logout or Rotation
payload_types.description: Attacker captures a valid token and replays it after the user has logged out, the token has been rotated, or the session has been revoked — exploiting stateless JWT designs that lack server-side revocation.
examples: Intercepting a JWT before logout and replaying it after the user's session is terminated — server still accepts it until natural expiry
Capturing a refresh token from a compromised device and using it to obtain new access tokens after the user resets their password
Replaying an OAuth access token stolen from browser localStorage after the application issues a replacement token
Reusing an API key that was rotated but not immediately invalidated in the grace period window
Replaying a token from a previous authentication session that has the same claims as the current session, bypassing re-authentication requirements
mitigation: Implement a server-side token revocation list (blocklist or jti claim registry) checked on every request for sensitive operations even with short-lived tokens.
Use short access token lifetimes (≤15 minutes) combined with one-time-use rotating refresh tokens — invalidate the refresh token immediately upon use.
On password change, email change, or logout, immediately invalidate all active tokens and refresh tokens for that user.
Track and bind tokens to device fingerprints or IP ranges for high-sensitivity APIs, rejecting replays from unexpected contexts.
Alert on token reuse from multiple geographic locations or IP addresses simultaneously as a token theft indicator.

payload_types.description: Attacker submits large volumes of username/password combinations or leaked credential pairs against login and token issuance endpoints to gain unauthorized access through volume rather than exploiting a specific vulnerability.
examples: Automated script submitting 10,000 username/password combinations from a leaked credential database against POST /api/auth/token
Distributed credential stuffing attack using rotating residential proxies to bypass per-IP rate limiting
Brute-forcing a 4-digit OTP endpoint with no rate limiting or lockout — 10,000 combinations exhausted in minutes
Dictionary attack against an API key endpoint using common patterns (test123, apikey1, changeme) with no lockout
Password spraying: submitting one common password (e.g., Password1!) against every user account to avoid per-account lockout thresholds
mitigation: Apply progressive account lockout on authentication endpoints: warn after 5 failed attempts, temporary lock after 10, full lock after 20 — with unlock requiring email verification.
Implement per-IP and per-account rate limiting with CAPTCHA challenges triggered after threshold failures on all authentication endpoints.
Integrate leaked credential databases (HaveIBeenPwned API) to reject passwords known to appear in public breach datasets at registration and password change time.
Deploy behavioral anomaly detection to identify credential-stuffing patterns: high failure rate, regular timing intervals, many different usernames from the same IP.
Require MFA for all accounts — credential stuffing is neutralized when the correct password alone is insufficient for access.

payload_types.description: Attacker exploits weaknesses in OAuth 2.0 flows — including authorization code interception, token leakage via referrer headers, implicit flow token theft, or insufficient scope validation — to obtain or abuse access tokens.
examples: Authorization code interception via open redirect: attacker registers a redirect_uri matching a wildcard or open redirect on the legitimate domain
Access token leaked in browser history or Referer header when the implicit flow includes the token in the URL fragment
Missing state parameter validation on the OAuth callback enables CSRF attacks that link a victim's account to an attacker-controlled identity
Scope escalation: exchanging an authorization code for tokens with broader scopes than those originally requested by the user
Client secret exposed in a mobile app's binary — used to impersonate the legitimate OAuth client and request tokens
mitigation: Enforce exact redirect_uri matching — never use prefix or wildcard matching; reject any redirect_uri not pre-registered for the specific client_id.
Use the Authorization Code flow with PKCE for all clients (especially mobile and SPA) — never use the implicit flow which exposes tokens in URLs.
Validate the state parameter on every OAuth callback to prevent CSRF token binding attacks.
Enforce the principle of least privilege for OAuth scopes — only issue tokens with the minimum scopes explicitly requested and approved; never escalate silently.
Store client secrets in a secrets vault and never embed them in client-side code, mobile binaries, or public repositories.

payload_types.type: Weak or Exposed API Key Authentication
payload_types.description: API keys with insufficient entropy, no expiry, excessive scope, or exposed in client-side code or logs provide attackers with persistent, hard-to-detect authentication bypass.
examples: API key committed to a public GitHub repository — discovered and used by attacker within minutes of exposure
API key embedded in a mobile app's APK binary, extractable via decompilation
API key transmitted in a URL query parameter (?apikey=abc123) — logged by CDN, proxy, and browser history
Single API key with full read/write/admin scope used across multiple integrations — compromise of any one exposes all
API keys generated with insufficient entropy (sequential integers or short alphanumeric strings) are guessable through brute force
mitigation: Generate API keys with at least 256 bits of cryptographically random entropy — never use sequential or predictable formats.
Transmit API keys exclusively in the Authorization header (Bearer scheme), never in URL query parameters, request bodies, or cookies.
Implement API key scoping: issue separate keys per integration with only the minimum required permissions, so compromise of one key does not grant broad access.
Set mandatory expiry on all API keys and implement automated rotation with alerts for keys approaching expiry — never issue non-expiring keys.
Scan source code repositories, CI/CD pipelines, and container images continuously for accidental API key exposure using secret detection tools (GitGuardian, truffleHog, AWS Macie).

payload_types.description: Attacker fixes a known session identifier before authentication, hijacks session cookies transmitted over unencrypted connections, or exploits insecure cookie attributes to take over authenticated sessions.
examples: Session fixation: attacker sets a known session ID via a URL parameter before the victim logs in — session is promoted to authenticated without re-issuance
Session cookie transmitted over HTTP (no Secure flag) — intercepted on a public Wi-Fi network via passive eavesdropping
Cookie missing HttpOnly flag — stolen via JavaScript XSS and sent to an attacker-controlled endpoint
Overly broad cookie domain (domain=.example.com) allows a session from a compromised subdomain to be replayed against the main application
Long-lived session cookies (30-day expiry, no rotation) stolen from a device and replayed weeks later
mitigation: Issue a new session identifier immediately after successful authentication — never promote a pre-authentication session ID to an authenticated state.
Set the Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Strict attributes on all session cookies — Secure prevents interception, HttpOnly prevents JavaScript access, SameSite prevents CSRF.
Enforce HTTPS on all endpoints with HSTS (including preload) to prevent session cookies from ever being transmitted over unencrypted connections.
Implement session binding to user-agent and IP subnet — flag and terminate sessions that change these properties mid-session without re-authentication.
Set appropriate session timeouts: short idle timeout (15–30 min) with absolute maximum session lifetime, and require re-authentication for any sensitive action.

how_to_test: Step 1: Call protected endpoints with no Authorization header — assert 401 Unauthorized is returned with no data
Step 2: Submit an expired JWT and a JWT with a tampered payload claim — assert both are rejected with 401
Step 3: Test alg:none by stripping the signature and setting alg to none in the JWT header — assert the server rejects it
Step 4: Replay a valid token after logout — call the logout endpoint, then immediately reuse the captured token and assert 401
Step 5: Attempt brute force on the login endpoint — submit 20+ failed attempts and verify rate limiting or lockout engages
Step 6: Test OAuth callback with a missing or incorrect state parameter — assert the callback is rejected
Step 7: Inspect all API responses and error messages to confirm no tokens, session IDs, or credentials appear in logs or responses
expected_outcomes: Requests with missing, expired, or tampered tokens return 401 — never partial data or a different error code
Tokens with alg:none or unexpected algorithms are unconditionally rejected
Replayed tokens after logout are rejected even if not yet naturally expired
Brute-force attempts trigger rate limiting or account lockout before exhausting the credential space
All authentication failure events are logged with user identifier, IP address, and timestamp for security monitoring
minimal_example: GET /profile without Authorization → 401; with expired JWT → 401; with alg:none JWT → 401; with valid token lacking scope → 403
mitigation: Enforce OAuth 2.0 / OIDC with short-lived JWTs (≤15 min access tokens, rotating refresh tokens) and strict claim validation (iss, aud, exp, kid) — explicitly whitelist accepted algorithms and reject alg:none unconditionally

Implement adaptive MFA for sensitive operations and require step-up re-authentication for account changes, payment actions, or privilege escalation; implement server-side token revocation checked on every request for sensitive endpoints

Add brute-force and credential-stuffing defenses on all auth endpoints: progressive account lockout, CAPTCHA after failed attempts, IP-based rate limiting, and leaked-credential database checks at registration

Enforce TLS across all connections with HSTS; prohibit credentials in URLs or log output; set Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite=Strict on all session cookies; rotate API keys on a fixed schedule with automated expiry alerts
